text: In studies of scientific creativity, Kevin Dunbar observed laboratory meetings where breakthroughs rarely emerged from solitary late-night work — they came from conversations in which an analogy from one scientist's field illuminated a problem in another's. He called this distributed cognition: ideas incubate not only inside individual minds but in the space between them. This suggests the creative process is not strictly sequential. Incubation can be social as well as solitary. A colleague's offhand remark can trigger the illumination that weeks of private effort failed to produce, because the external perspective supplies the conceptual distance that time alone is meant to create.
question: What does Dunbar's concept of distributed cognition suggest about the incubation phase?

options.text: It is most effective when individuals work in complete isolation.
It requires a minimum of several weeks to produce genuine insight.
It depends on unconscious memory consolidation during sleep.
Incubation can occur socially, not only inside an individual mind.
